task: narrate
chapter: 6.Historical Knowledge and Its Practical Application
topic: Grade 10: Local Food
source: From ancient time the people of this country consumed things which grew in the country. Evidence has been discovered on wild breadfruit and Atikesel which were consumed by the pre historic people who lived in the Beligala cave 10500 years ago. In the historical era paddy and Kurakkan were profusely used for food. Apart from these, Undu, green gram (Mun), sesame (Thala), marrow (Mae), Amu, Menari, Kongu, Waru, Yawa were also eaten. The historical books of Sri Lanka mention seven types of grain, which were used as food by ancient people. They are namely Sali, Veehi, Kedesa, Varaka, Kangu, Duma and Yawa. The Thonigala inscription, made by king Keerthi Sri Meghavarnabhaya (301 -328 A.D.), mentions Paddy, Undu, Mun (green gram) and Thala (sesame). Saddharmarathnawaliya mentions that the meat of peacock cooked in ghee and El rice (El Hal) was one of the most favourite foods among the chieftains in Sri Lanka in the 12th century A.D. Pala Maluwa and Kadi Embula with Niwudu rice was a most frequent meal of poor people in that time. Kady Embula is made of stale rice powder. Ancient Sri Lankans had a habit of drinking various types of gruels (Kenda). The words Yagu or Hambu had been used
target_text: Ancient Sri Lankans ate natural, local foods. Prehistoric (10,500 years ago): Wild breadfruit, Atikesel (Beligala cave). Historical era staples: Paddy, Kurakkan; grains like Undu, Mun, Thala, Mae, Amu, Menari, Kongu, Waru, Yawa; seven grains (Sali, Veehi, Kedesa, Varaka, Kangu, Duma, Yawa). Thonigala inscription (Keerthi Sri Meghavarnabhaya, 301328 AD): Paddy, Undu, Mun, Thala. Chieftains favorites: Peacock meat in ghee, El Hal rice (12th century). Poor meals: Pala Maluwa, Kadi Embula with Niwudu rice. Desserts: Thala Guli (sesame balls). Yams: Wel Ala, Kukulala, Katu Ala, Kondola, Bathala, Sondala. Vegetables: Wetakolu, Thampala, Weraniya Dalu, Kara Kola, Lunuwila Kola, Labu, Puhul, Karabatu, Kekiri, Thibbatu, Nelambu Ala, Murunga. Cooking: Bedum (fry), Mellum (half-cooked leaves), Hodda (gravy), Anama,Embula,Niyambalawa curries. Spices (Kulu Badu,Thuna Paha): Lunu, Duru, Miris + Sudu,Kalu Duru, Koththamalli, Aba, Uluhal (or Asamodagam,Sathakuppa). Sours: Dehi, Dodam, Goraka, Nasnaran, Gadadehi; Karapincha. Oils: Dunthel, Githel (ghee), less Polthel. Dairy: Milk,curd common (Kirigeri cows). Gruels (Kenda,Yagu,Hambu,Kaadi): Diya Kenda (salty), Yawagu,Ulu Kenda (medicinal), Rasa Ulu Kenda (sweet with milk,honey,ghee,orange), Embul Kenda (herbal). Meat,fish: Land animals, fish (tax Mathrika Mathsya Bhagaya). Natural, nutritious diet for healthy farming life.

task: narrate
chapter: 7. Decline of Ancient Cities in the Dry Zone and Origin of New Kingdoms in South West
topic: Grade 10: The First Urbanization
source: By 450 B.C. the social stratification of our country had become complete to a certain extent. Early inscriptions substantiate that there were a large number of chieftains who owned production surplus, spread in the dry zone by that time. The number of the persons introduced by the name of Parumaka in the inscriptions so far is numerous. These chieftains had been organized as families. One of the strategies that these families used to show and keep their power and prestige was using articles imported from foreign countries. Some articles such as imported earthenware and beads have been discovered from excavations done in the ancient cities such as Anuradhapura and Tissamaharama. These things were exchanged as a result of joining the foreign trade. As Sri Lanka participated actively in the international trade activities operating then in the Indian Ocean, an immense wealth came into the country. The minority who was the owners of the production surplus were able to live a luxurious life as they were free from other external problems. Since the influx of income needed to be administered, the requirement of a mechanism of administration was felt. T he groups who became the chieftains
target_text: By 450 BC, social stratification grew from surplus-owning Parumaka chieftains in the dry zone, displaying wealth via imported goods (earthenware,beads from Anuradhapura,Tissamaharama excavations). Indian Ocean trade brought riches, enabling luxurious lives for elites and needing administrationleading to planned cities with markets, temples, parks, moats, tanks. Pandukabhaya designed Anuradhapura (Mahawamsa), self-sufficient for ~1400 years via farming, iron, irrigation; Magama mirrored southern culture. Post-6th century AD, Arab trade shifted east; after 9th century, focus on Southeast Asia,China prompted Vijayabahu I to choose Polonnaruwa near eastern ports. Parakramabahu I era showed Southeast Asian influence: royal marriages (Burma,Cambodia), art like Gal Viharaya Buddhas and Sathmahal Prasada.

source: When the prosperous cities existing in the dry zone declined, several cities, especially outside the dry zone, emerged in the South-eastern areas in no time. The emergence of these cities is a result of another process of urbanization. Operating several ruling centres due to decentralization of political power, growth of new harbours, reflecting a new intelligence awakening due to the rebirth of literature provide a sufficient base to introduce this process as a second urbanization. Another important trend that should be taken into consideration regarding the advancement of urbanization during this time is setting up of new trade relations network and widening of commercial activities with that. When we compare the cities originated in the second urbanization process with the great cities that existed in the previous era, the main feature that could be seen is that the plans of the cities developed in the second urbanization had freed from the geometric structures of the previous ones. Even in the size, they were not as large as the Anuradhapura and Polonnaruwa ones. The scholars point out the reason for that is the decentralization of power after the second urbanization. task: narrate
chapter: 4. The Ancient Society of Sri Lanka
topic: Grade 10: The Nature of Ruling
source: The historical sources say that kings ruled our country in the ancient times. The inscriptions mention very important information about the procedure of the statesmanship followed in ruling the country. There are three institutions which handle the ruling of any country. They are called the legislature, the executive and the judiciary. The government means these three institutions. The legislature is the institution which makes laws in a country. As it is done by the parliament of our country today, in the ancient time it was done by the kings court. The executive is the institution which implements laws. The government officers are engaged in that process. The judiciary provides the justice to the countrymen by preventing the damage caused to the society due to breach of law. In the ancient time there were an executive as well as a judiciary in our country. The executive acted as the legislature too. There was not an independent body as a separate legislature, because then there was monarchism in this country. The inscriptions written in 9th century A.D. had used the word Ekthensamiya to name the executive and the legislature. In an inscription in the place called Buddhannehela
target_text: In ancient Sri Lanka, kings ruled through a centralized system combining legislature, executive, and judiciary, with the kings court (Ekthensamiya) acting as both lawmaker and executor, as seen in 9th-century inscriptions like Buddhannehela, where land grants required royal decrees. The judiciary (Sabhawa), represented by officers like Udaya and Sena, handled legal land transfers, as in Anuradhapuras Kerelegama grant to Mahindarama nunnery. Secretaries (Mahale), like Kashyapa under King Sena II, documented state affairs. Provincial administration involved ministers, like Rishigiri in Nagadeepa under Vasabha, and local councils (Dasagam Ettan) managed ten villages, resolving issues like temple alms at Kaludiya Pokuna. Kings prioritized public welfare, building hospitals (Vejjasala) since the 2nd century BC, with Buddhadasa (340368 AD) and Mahinda V (9821029 AD) expanding them, Kashyapa IV (898914 AD) creating a fever hospital (Upasaggaroganasa), and Upatissa I (365406 AD) establishing maternity hospitals (Thimbirige). Kings maintained foreign relations with India, China, Rome, and Arabia, fostering trade and diplomacy, such as Gajabahu Is (114136 AD) visit to Chera, Bhatikabhayas (23 BC7 AD) mission to Rome, Aggabodhi VIs 20 missions to China, and Vijayabahu Is (10701110 AD) marital alliances with Kalinga and Pandya, alongside hiring Veleikkara mercenaries, ensuring peace, welfare, and territorial integrity.

chapter: 10. Sri Lanka and the Western World
topic: Grade 10: Arrival of the Portuguese in Asia
source: There were three main routes through which Asia and Europe continued their trade relations from the distant past. 1. The Silk Route which led from the city of Constantinople through Persia, the Central Asia to China. 2. The route which led from Constantinople to Egypt through the Mediterranean Sea, Red Sea to the Indian Ocean. 3. The route which led from Constantinople through Bagdad, the Persian Gulf to the Western coast of India. They used ships in the sea and the caravans on land to transport the commodities when they made their trade relations. By the mediaeval period, the monopoly of trades in Asia was in the hands of the Muslims. The Muslims made a great profit by collecting the commodities including spices in Asia and selling them to Europeans. As all three trade routes above mentioned had connected with the city of Constantinople, that city which was under the European Christian believers, had become the main centre of the East and West trading. But, when this city was captured by the Turks who followed the Islam religion in 1453, it was a great blow to the traditional trading between Asia and Europe. The European Christians suffered a great loss, due to the capture of the
target_text: Three ancient trade routes linked Asia-Europe: Silk Route (land via Persia,Central Asia), Mediterranean-Red Sea-Indian Ocean, Baghdad-Persian Gulf-India. Muslims controlled Asian trade by mediaeval times, profiting from spices sold to Europeans via Constantinople. Ottoman Turks captured Constantinople (1453), blocking routes, raising pricespushing Europeans to find new sea paths. Portugal, Atlantic-facing and seafaring, led under Prince Henry the Navigators school (navigation,astronomy). Vasco da Gama reached Calicut (1498) rounding Africa, starting 450-year European dominance (Portuguese, Dutch, English, French). Portuguese goals: Economic (seize Muslim spice monopoly) + religious (spread Catholicism, defeat Muslims). Vasco da Gama: We seek Christians and spices. Later fleets built warehouses, sent priests.

chapter: 3. Evolution of Political Power in Sri Lanka.
topic: Grade 10: Pre-state Era
source: According to the interpretation given above, the foundation of a state in the Sri Lankan history developed at a later time. It has been described in the next parts of this lesson. Here, the attention has been paid to explain the nature of the ruling system that existed in Sri Lanka before it became a state. That period is called pre-state era. T here was no one definite ruler to rule this country during the pre-state period. Instead, a group of wealthy chieftains dominated their power over small areas. At the beginning of writing inscriptions in this country these chieftains were introduced in them by the name of Parumaka. The word Parumaka is derived from the Sanskrit word Pramukha. Its meaning is main. They were given the power to rule a large or a small area based on the power they possessed as a result of their wealth. T here is an inscription, which assists us to show that the Parumakas had a certain provincial power, in Kothgalakanda in the Kandalama area close to Dambulla. That inscription belongs to 250 B.C. A Parumaka who offered a cave to the bikkus has been introduced as Thota Bojaka. Its meaning is Theerthaya Anubhava Karanna. Theerthaya means the port.

The major change that occurred as they got used to agriculture was that they started to stay in one place for a long time. This was the basic reason for the origin of the most ancient villages in this country. Those who lived in these basic villages were engaged in agriculture and animal husbandry as their livelihood. They engaged in hunting too. Due to agriculture and animal husbandry their food safety was ensured. Unless those people, who were engaged in those
target_text: Sri Lankas first cities emerged around 450 BC in Anuradhapura (North Central dry zone) and Magama (South,Southeast), showing urban populations though without full walls,moats,streets yet. From proto-historic era (~2400 BC), people shifted from nomadic hunting to settled agriculture and animal husbandry, forming villages. Fertile land and good water yielded surplus grain (Athirikthaya), creating wealthy farmers who became village leaders (like Parumaka). Poor harvests left some borrowing paddy, as in Jathaka Attakatha story. This economic divide (rich,poor) led to social stratification, a key driver of urbanization.

task: narrate
chapter: 2. Ancient Settlements
topic: Grade 10: Settlements in the Proto-historic Era
source: The period between the end of the prehistoric era and the beginning of the historic era is named as the proto-historic era. Technologically this was an era of transition. The proto-historic era in Sri Lanka is differentiated from the prehistoric era that existed in Sri Lanka before proto-historic era on the basis of several facts. Some practices such as using burnt pots and following a methodical burial system are prominent among them. Using iron instead of the technology of stone tools existed during the prehistoric era, was started during this period. However, using stone tools was completely given up during the final stage of the proto-historic era. This age is called the proto-historic Iron Age as well as the early Iron Age, as using of iron began in this era. And also the beginning of building permanent settlements and starting agriculture are some more transformations that could be seen in this period. The Origin of the Proto-historic Era T he knowledge that we possess at present on the transitory period between the beginning of the proto-historic era and the end of the prehistoric era in Sri Lanka is limited. The changes such as dying down of the technology of stone tools, h
target_text: The time between the prehistoric era and the historic era is called the proto-historic era. In Sri Lanka, this was a period of important changes. People began to use fired clay pots, organize burials, and slowly replace stone tools with iron tools. Because iron started to be used during this time, it is also called the proto-historic Iron Age or early Iron Age. People also began to live in permanent houses and started farming. These changes did not happen suddenly. Hunting and gathering slowly became less important, plant food became more common, and iron use increased over time. Experts believe that long-term climate changes in South Asia may have helped cause these cultural changes. Warmer conditions after the Ice Age supported farming, population growth, and social development. The earliest evidence of the proto-historic era in Sri Lanka dates to about 2400 B.C. Important evidence has been found in areas such as north-eastern Sabaragamuwa and south-western Uva. Megalithic burials are one of the main sources used to study this culture. These include stone tombs covered with large slabs and burial pots containing human remains. Sites such as Ibbankatuwa, Galsohonkanaththa, and Yatigalpoththa date from about 750 to 450 B.C. Other burial methods included urn burials and cremation burials in clay tubs. During this period, people moved away from a hunting life and began to build permanent settlements. This way of living is called sedentism. Evidence for this includes house remains. For example, a rectangular house found at Udaranchamadama in Ratnapura had divided sections, wattle-and-daub walls, and a thatched roof. Inside it, archaeologists found potsherds, some painted in red and white and made using the potters wheel. Stone and iron tools were also found, showing that iron technology was still new. Animal bones found there show that hunting still continued along with farming. Other discoveries such as clay beads, iron kohl sticks, ochre stones, and grinding stones show that people used body decoration and had developed useful skills. In this era, technology changed from mainly stone tools to metal tools and pottery. Iron tools helped people clear forests, dig the land, and farm more effectively. Pottery became more advanced, and house construction also improved. These changes show that proto-historic people in Sri Lanka were developing a more settled and organized way of life.

topic: Grade 10: Local Rituals
source: Though Buddhism was famous among people, at the time, they tended to do other practices (Yathukarma) too. Performing Poojas for various provincial gods was prominent among them. Apart from this, a simple practice called Kempahan was performed to solve day- to-day problems. What they do normally here is that performing a Pooja by lighting one votive lamp or several lamps with a certain oil (Mee or Sesame). This method was followed by our ancient people to prevent the threat of insects to their crops or to cure some diseases infected to cattle. In 2009 the archaeologists managed to find significant information about the Poojas performed for gods in the ancient time. They discovered some ruins of an ancient Devala in the excavations carried out in the area named Galpaya in Rathnapura district. Clay pots were kept upside down in a circle of about 6 feet diameter. There were marks of a fire of its inside space. T here were a clay water pot and a jaw of an ox. The archaeologists were able to find holes of posts which belonged to a hut which had been built to cover everything. In later analysis, it was revealed that the place had been used to perform Kirimadu Shantikarmaya. It is perf
target_text: Even after Buddhism spread widely, ancient Sri Lankans kept folk rituals (Yathukarma), offering Poojas to local gods and performing simple rites like Kempahanlighting oil lamps (Mee or sesame) to ward off crop pests or heal sick cattle. In 2009, excavations at Galpaya (Ratnapura) uncovered a 3rd-century AD Devala: upside-down clay pots in a 6-foot circle around a fire pit, with a water pot and ox jaw under a hut structuresite of Kirimadu Shantikarmaya for cattle protection, a ritual still practiced today in Uva and Sabaragamuwa provinces.

chapter: 7. Decline of Ancient Cities in the Dry Zone and Origin of New Kingdoms in South West
topic: Grade 10: The Second Urbanization
source: When the prosperous cities existing in the dry zone declined, several cities, especially outside the dry zone, emerged in the South-eastern areas in no time. The emergence of these cities is a result of another process of urbanization. Operating several ruling centres due to decentralization of political power, growth of new harbours, reflecting a new intelligence awakening due to the rebirth of literature provide a sufficient base to introduce this process as a second urbanization. Another important trend that should be taken into consideration regarding the advancement of urbanization during this time is setting up of new trade relations network and widening of commercial activities with that. When we compare the cities originated in the second urbanization process with the great cities that existed in the previous era, the main feature that could be seen is that the plans of the cities developed in the second urbanization had freed from the geometric structures of the previous ones. Even in the size, they were not as large as the Anuradhapura and Polonnaruwa ones. The scholars point out the reason for that is the decentralization of power after the second urbanization. Dambadeni
target_text: After Polonnaruwa's fall (1215 AD), power decentralized, leading to second urbanization in smaller, non-geometric cities outside the dry zone: Dambadeniya (12321270s AD): Vijayabahu III (12321236) founded, brought Tooth Relic; Parakramabahu II (12361270) defeated Magha (~1255), Chandrabhanu (1247, 1261 with Pandyan aid). Yapahuwa (1270s1280s): Buvanekabahu I shifted for safety, traded with Egypt (1283 ambassadors). Kurunegala (12931341): Buvanekabahu II, Parakramabahu IV (literature revival, Jataka translation). Gampola (13411415): Buvanekabahu IV founded; matriarchal succession (uncle-nephew); Alakeshwara family strong; Chinese invasions (Chen Ho 14051411 captured Veera Alakeshwara). Kotte (14151597): Parakramabahu VI (14151467) united country, conquered Jaffna (Sapumal), brought Vanni under rule. Jaffna Kingdom (post-Rajarata, founder Arya Chakravarthi); Vanniyars: 18 small units in low-density areas, semi-independent but under stronger kings.

topic: Grade 10: Law and Tradition
source: The common principles formed by a society are very important to run a society. Those common principles later become the law. The principles which are formed by a society are of two types. Some of them are systems of written laws. Other social acceptances are traditional. The law and tradition control the anti-social behaviours of people for the benefit of the society. To protect the goodness of the society our intelligent rulers acted in accordance with law and tradition from ancient time. Our kings acted with good understanding to make laws, implement those laws and to supervise those laws when they are used practically. In the 9th century A.D this country was ruled by a royal lineage which belonged to the dynasty called Manawamma. The inscriptions inscribed by those kings, mention about law institutions of that time. In those inscriptions the two words Wadala and Sabhawa are found. In legal activities officers from Wadala and Sabhawa engaged in them. Here the word Wadala means the 'legislature', which is the parliament. In ancient time it was the kings court. In those inscriptions the word Sabhawa means the judiciary. The laws imposed in a country are implemented by
target_text: Ancient Sri Lankan kings balanced written laws and traditions for social harmony. Governance: Kings court (Wadala) made laws; judiciary (Sabhawa) enforced them; security officers (Mekapper,Dandanayaka) implemented, as in Panakaduwa plate. Taxes: Dakapathi (water), Bojakapathi (kings land), 1,6 produce or 1,10 nuts; customs at harbors (Godawaya inscription donated fees to temple). Environmental laws: Fines for untimely ploughing (soil erosion, Kondawattawan); crop restrictions (Wessagiriya, Mahinda IV). Trade laws (Sorabora Wewa, Udaya III): Tax market traders only, double for hidden goods, standard weights, sheltered betel, Poya day oil fine for temple. Animal protection: Amandagamini Abhayas no-kill law; Mihindu IVs death for cattle,goat killing; Nissankamalla banned bird hunting in tanks. Criminal punishments (Wewalkaetiya, Mihindu IV): Hanging for murder, branding cattle thieves, village tax (Gruhadanda) until capture. Punishments deterred crime, upholding justice.

The government officers are engaged in that process. The judiciary provides the justice to the countrymen by preventing the damage caused to the society due to breach of law. In the ancient time there were an executive as well as a judiciary in our country. The executive acted as the legislature too. There was not an independent body as a separate legislature, because then there was monarchism in this country. source: History is a subject which studies the past human activities. When we study the history of hundreds and thousands of years back, we find writings and various creations made by people who lived in those ages. They are called sources because information about past human activities can be extracted from them. The sources, which belonged to each different age, provide us a good understanding about those eras and their social backgrounds. Thus, when the information taken from those sources are studied logically, the history about peoples lives in each period is formed. As there are a number of sources which are available to study history, they are mainly divided into two; 1. Literary sources 2. Archaeological sources Books and documents written at different time periods by the people who lived in the past are considered literary sources. Relics, monuments, coins, inscriptions and various ruins which are unearthed from archaeological excavations and experiments are called archaeological sources. target_text: In Sri Lankas dry zone, ancient engineers mastered water management to support agriculture and cities through massive tanks, bunds, sluices, canals, and fountains. Tank bunds were built on rock between hills (e.g., Yodha Wewa bund 7 miles long, 44 ft high), layered with compacted clay, soil, gravel, and kirimeti for strength, beaten by elephants. Ralapanawa stone linings protected bunds from wave erosion. Sluices (Sorowwa) with stone slabs (Diyaketa Pahana) and Bisokotuwa valves controlled pressure release, reducing flood risk, with mud sluices clearing silt. Canals like Elahera (from Amban Ganga to Minneriya, Giritale, etc.) and Minipe diverted rivers. Monastic ponds (Kuttam Pokuna, Eth Pokuna) and clay-lined wells (Uraketa Lin) ensured clean water. Sigiriyas fountains used natural pressure from 600 ft height, with adjustable stone orifices varying jet height, spiral gutters for sedimentation, and filtersstill working in rain today.

topic: Grade 10: Protecting Archaeological Sources
source: When we consider the long history of Sri Lanka, expansion of ancient population, and the number of people who had lived in this country, what remains here for us as archaeological sources today is only the creations made of long lasting raw materials such as stone, metal and clay; or large scale creations such as tanks and canals. T here is a possibility of these valuable sources getting destroyed due to speedy urbanization, expansion of settlements, constructing roads, constructing buildings, and agricultural activities happening in the country. Today we see historical places, monuments and antiquities because our ancestors had protected them. Therefore, it is a responsibility of the citizens of the country to protect them as the present generation. T here are different types of ancient marks inscribed on the rocky surfaces in various areas in Sri Lanka. Among them various figures such as, Sun and Moon, Palmyra- fan, bow, arrow, conch, ox-hoof, tusker- step, figures of various animals are marks that show the ancient borders of areas; or they are an ancient form of communication as the present road signs. Some people dig out and destroy the places where there are these marks and mo
target_text: Sri Lanka has a very long history, but only some remains of the past have survived until today. These archaeological sources are usually made from strong materials like stone, metal, and clay, or they are large creations such as tanks and canals. Today, these valuable sources are in danger because of rapid urbanization, new settlements, road building, construction work, and farming activities. We are able to see historical places, monuments, and antiquities today because our ancestors protected them. In the same way, it is our duty to protect them for the future. In different parts of Sri Lanka, there are ancient marks carved on rock surfaces. These include symbols like the sun, moon, palmyra fan, bow, arrow, conch, ox hoof, elephant step, and animal figures. These may have been used to mark borders or to communicate, like road signs today. Some people damage these places, moonstones, guard stones, stone pillars, and other antiquities because they wrongly think treasures are buried there. Also, writing and drawing on historical places damages them. Such acts are against the law and can be punished. Therefore, students must understand the value of antiquities and help protect them.

task: narrate
chapter: 4. The Ancient Society of Sri Lanka
topic: Grade 10: Economy
source: The majority of the people lived in villages. In comparison to the present condition, those villagers lives were shaped up in a very simple way. Most of their occupation was agriculture. Some were engaged in Chena cultivation whereas the others were engaged in paddy cultivation. They reared animals for living. Apart from these, some, who were engaged in different occupations, lived in cities. 4.2.1 Farming, Agriculture T he main livelihood of the villagers who lived in the ancient Sri Lanka was farming. Animal husbandry or rearing animals was also done in line with the agricultural activities. Agriculture was done in two ways; one was muddy agriculture alias paddy cultivation; second was Chena cultivation. The historical and archaeological sources substantiate that the Chena cultivation was the oldest out of these two. The term Hena (Chena) used in ancient time has been mentioned as Sehen in Buthsarana, the literary text, Sen in Ummagga Jathaka, and Pitibim in inscriptions. Chena Cultivation  Hena or Chena cultivation is an inland cultivation. A suitable land for Hena or Chena is prepared for growing grains and vegetables after choosing a plot of land from a jungle ar
target_text: In ancient Sri Lanka, most people lived in simple villages, primarily engaged in agriculture, including Chena (slash-and-burn) and paddy cultivation, alongside animal husbandry. Chena cultivation, the older practice, involved clearing jungle plots to grow grains like finger millet, corn, and sesame, and vegetables like brinjal, with crops like sugarcane and cotton supporting jaggery and cloth production. Farmers lived in temporary huts (Pela) and used stick fences (Dandu Weta) to protect crops. King Nissankamalla (11871196 AD) abolished Chena taxes. Paddy cultivation, using irrigation from tanks and canals, occurred in Maha, Yala, and Meda seasons, with King Parakramabahu I (11531186 AD) emphasizing water conservation. Animal husbandry included cattle for milk, curd, ghee, and butter, and chickens and goats for meat, as seen in inscriptions like Medirigiriyas 9th-century hospital rules. Industries thrived, with blacksmiths (Kabara) making tools, goldsmiths (Thuladhara), gem artisans (Manikara), tusk carvers (Datika), potters (Kumbhakara), and weavers (Thathvaya) producing goods. Trade flourished in cities like Anuradhapura and Magama, with markets like Kalasumana and Hopitigama regulated by laws, such as taxing only market-bound carts and fining Poya day traders. Traders (Wanija,Vapara) and organizations (Poogaya) like Nanadesi and Cambodian Kajoba Maha Pugiya engaged in local and international trade via harbors like Mathota and Gokannathiththa. Professions included teachers (Acharya) for crafts like elephant taming, doctors (Veja,Maha Vedana), lawyers (Vohara), and artisans like dancers (Tissa), painters (Lapana Tissa), and poets (Sanjaya), contributing to a vibrant society.

task: narrate
chapter: 1.Sources of Studying History
topic: Grade 10: Classification of Sources
source: History is a subject which studies the past human activities. When we study the history of hundreds and thousands of years back, we find writings and various creations made by people who lived in those ages. They are called sources because information about past human activities can be extracted from them. The sources, which belonged to each different age, provide us a good understanding about those eras and their social backgrounds. Thus, when the information taken from those sources are studied logically, the history about peoples lives in each period is formed. As there are a number of sources which are available to study history, they are mainly divided into two; 1. Literary sources 2. Archaeological sources Books and documents written at different time periods by the people who lived in the past are considered literary sources. Relics, monuments, coins, inscriptions and various ruins which are unearthed from archaeological excavations and experiments are called archaeological sources. Next, lets discuss the above mentioned two types further using the sources which are helpful to study the history of Sri Lanka. 1. Literary Sources The literary sources, which can be used
target_text: History is the study of past human activities. We learn about the past through sources. Sources are things that give us information about how people lived long ago. Historical sources are mainly divided into two types: literary sources and archaeological sources. Literary sources are books, writings, and documents written by people in the past. Archaeological sources are physical remains such as relics, monuments, coins, inscriptions, and ruins. In Sri Lanka, literary sources can be divided into local and foreign sources. Local literary sources are texts written by Sri Lankans. Deepawamsa is the oldest known local literary source and was written in the 4th century A.D. It includes information about Sri Lanka and the Buddhist order up to the time of King Mahasen. Because Deepawamsa had some repeated and brief information, Mahawamsa was written with fewer mistakes. The first part of Mahawamsa was written by Mahanama Thero in the 5th or 6th century A.D. Later, other writers added more parts, so Mahawamsa became a continuous record of Sri Lankan history. It is considered a trustworthy source because some of its facts are supported by inscriptions and other evidence. Another important text is Wansattappakasini, which explains parts of the Mahawamsa and adds more information. Other local literary works such as Bodhiwamasa, Thupawamsa, Dhatuwamsa, Poojawaliya, Saddharmalankaraya, Nikaya Sangraha, and Rajawaliya are also useful for studying history. Poems such as Sandesha Kawya, Prashasthi Kawya, and Hatan Kawya are important too. When using literary sources, we must think about when they were written, who wrote them, why they were written, and how the writer got the information. Foreign literary sources are books written by foreigners who mentioned Sri Lanka. Some Indian and Tamil books also include information about Sri Lanka. These sources are useful for understanding trade, foreign relations, and outside views of the country. Literary sources help historians arrange events in order, study political, economic, and social life, compare information, and understand international relations. Archaeological sources include inscriptions, coins, ruins, drawings, sculptures, and antiquities. Inscriptions are writings found on stone, clay, copper plates, wood, and other materials. The oldest inscriptions in Sri Lanka are Brahmi inscriptions. They are important for understanding ancient society, settlements, trade, laws, and religion. Coins are another valuable source. The oldest coins in Sri Lanka are called Kahapana. Coins help us understand trade, economy, metal use, and foreign relations. Ancient ruins such as buildings, stupas, pillars, tanks, and ponds show the architecture, technology, and water management skills of ancient people. Drawings and sculptures help us understand clothing, jewellery, art, and religious beliefs. For example, the Sigiriya frescoes show the beauty culture and appearance of women in ancient Sri Lanka. Antiquities are objects used or made by ancient people and are found in museums, religious places, and excavations. They help us understand ancient culture, technology, lifestyle, and foreign connections. Archaeological sources are often more reliable because they usually belong to the same time as the historical events. If literary sources are supported by archaeological sources, the information becomes more believable. Therefore, historians must use all relevant sources carefully and compare them to construct accurate history.

chapter: 5.The Ancient Science and Technology in Sri Lanka
topic: Grade 10: Local Rituals
source: Though Buddhism was famous among people, at the time, they tended to do other practices (Yathukarma) too. Performing Poojas for various provincial gods was prominent among them. Apart from this, a simple practice called Kempahan was performed to solve day- to-day problems. What they do normally here is that performing a Pooja by lighting one votive lamp or several lamps with a certain oil (Mee or Sesame). This method was followed by our ancient people to prevent the threat of insects to their crops or to cure some diseases infected to cattle. In 2009 the archaeologists managed to find significant information about the Poojas performed for gods in the ancient time. They discovered some ruins of an ancient Devala in the excavations carried out in the area named Galpaya in Rathnapura district. Clay pots were kept upside down in a circle of about 6 feet diameter. In the ancient time there were an executive as well as a judiciary in our country. The executive acted as the legislature too. target_text: Second urbanization architecture blended South Indian styles: Yapahuwa steps,posts (Pandyan,Cambodian influence); Gadaladeniya Pilimageya (South Indian); Devalas attached to temples (e.g., Upulvan at Gadaladeniya, Lankathilaka) showing Hindu,Mahayana impact. Weaving clothes became a little faster with the invention of the flying shuttle by John K. in 1733. But the slowness in preparing threads needed in weaving clothes still remained a problem. According to the situation that was there at that time, threads produced by six thread makers per day were enough only for one weaver to work for one day. Therefore, there was some idea of increasing the pace of spinning. As a result of that enthusiasm, a person called James Hargreaves produced a machine called Jenny in 1764 for cotton weaving. T his machine was a manually operated one. Later in 1769, Richard Arkwright produced a more efficient, water powered spinning wheel. It was named as water turbine. Arkwrights creation was an important produc
target_text: Industrial Revolution in Britain centered on 3 industries: 1. Textile: Flying shuttle (John Kay 1733) sped weaving; Spinning Jenny (James Hargreaves 1764) for threads; Water frame (Richard Arkwright 1769) water-powered; Mule (Samuel Crompton 1779) finer threads; Steam power (Edmund Cartwright 1785). 2. Iron,Steel: Coal smelting (Abraham Darby 1730) for cast iron; Puddling,rolling (Henry Cort 1784) wrought iron; Bessemer process (Henry Bessemer 1856) cheap steel; Open-hearth (1860) high-quality steel; Electric arc furnace (William Siemens 1870). 3. Coal: Steam engine (Thomas Newcomen 1712, James Watt 1769 improved) for pumping,mining; Safety lamp (Humphry Davy 1815) for gas; Cable haulage (1839) replaced child labor. By-products: Tar (roads), dyes, chemicals. These fueled transport (railways), agriculture, communication.

source: The Kandyan kingdom was known by various names such as Senkadagala Nuwara, Udarata Rajadhaniya, Kande Nuwara, Mahanuwara Rajadhaniya. The capital of the Kanda Udarata Rajadhaniya was Kandy (Mahanuwara). It is a naturally protected location. The city of Kandy is protected by the river of Mahaweli. It is very difficult to access this city due to the ghats such as Balana and Hunnasgiriya. Further, the Kandyan kingdom consists of a balmy climate and a fertile land. King Senasammatha Vickramabahu made Kandy an independent kingdom for the first time. It was a provincial ruling unit under the Kotte kingdom during the ruling period of king Parakramabahu VI. Therefore, King Senasammatha Vickramabahu is considered as the founder of the Kandyan kingdom. The successor of king Senasammatha Vickramabahu was his son, Jayaweera Bandara (1511-1551). He ruled the kingdom, for a long time. He was a friend of king Mayadunne, who was the ruler of Seethawaka. In the incident Vijayaba Kollaya that took place against king Vijayabahu VI, who was the then ruler of Kotte, king Jayaweera Bandara supported king Mayadunne. After King Jayaweera Bandara, his son Karaliyedde Bandara acceded to the throne of Kand
target_text: Nestled in misty hills, ringed by Mahaweli River and guarded by Balana,Hunnasgiriya passes, Kandy (Senkadagala,Mahanuwara) became an independent kingdom under Senasammatha Vickramabahu (~early 16th century), previously a Kotte province. Jayaweera Bandara (15111551) allied with Sitawakas Mayadunne against Kottes Vijayaba Kollaya. Karaliyadde Bandaras pro-Portuguese policy led chieftains to back Sitawakas Rajasinghe I, who captured Kandy (1580), killing Veerasundara Bandara. Portuguese installed puppet Yamasinghe Bandara (Don Philip), but Konappu Bandara (Veerasundaras son) seized power as Vimaladharmasooriya I (15921604), founding a new dynasty, marrying Dona Katharina, reclaiming Tooth Relic, defeating Portuguese at Danture (1594), reviving Buddhism (Upasampada from Myanmar), and boosting economy (agriculture, cotton, iron). Senarath (16041635) married Dona Katharina, divided kingdom among sons to avoid rivalry. Rajasinghe II (16351687) crushed Portuguese at Gannoruwa (1638), allied with Dutch to expel them (1658), recaptured lost lands (16701675), gaining ports like Batticaloa. Vimaladharmasooriya II (16871707) restored Tooth Relic temple, revived Upasampada from Myanmar. Narendrasinghe (17071739) faced chieftain power growth. Nayakkar dynasty began with Sri Vijaya Rajasinghe (17391747), Keerthi Sri Rajasinghe (17471782) revived Siyam Nikaya with Weliwita Saranankara, Rajadhi Rajasinghe (17821798), and Sri Vickrama Rajasinghe (17981815), whose cruelty alienated chieftains, leading to British capture (1815) and exile to Vellore.

target_text: Ancient Sri Lankan architecture blended science and artistry in stupas, palaces, and cities. Lightning conductors (Vajra Chumbata) protected tall stupas like Ruwanweli Seya from strikes. Ruwanweli Seyas foundation (King Dutugemunu) used layered concrete-like technology: deep pit, limestone crushed by elephants, Wendaru clay, gravel, crystal, iron net with gum, and oil-soaked Ransiriyal stones for durability. Sigiriya (5th century AD) featured mathematical planning: 200m rock palace (3 ha top), 4090 ha parks, 10 km walls, 8 km moats. Hygiene: Maternity houses (Sivikasala,Pandukabhaya), hospitals (18 by Dutugemunu), Polonnaruwa hospital ruins (147.5×109.2 ft) with surgical tools; deep-pit lavatories and drainage gutters prevented pollution. Iron technology: From 2400 BC (Beragala slag), wind-powered furnaces at Samanalawewa (3rd century BC) using monsoon winds for 12-hour smelting of Limonite,Hematite with Maran wood; bellows at Dehigaha-elakanda (180 BC200 AD). Minor industries: Tiny golden pendants (21 carvings on paddy-seed size, Jetavanaya), colored roof tiles (Abhayagiriya), advanced forging for weapons,armor.

When we compare the cities originated in the second urbanization process with the great cities that existed in the previous era, the main feature that could be seen is that the plans of the cities developed in the second urbanization had freed from the geometric structures of the previous ones. source: History is a subject which is linked with the study of sources. It could be studied with curiosity too. T herefore, one who studies history naturally tends to read books. Since the reader comes across with diverse facts, ideas, and opinions in reading books, his or her critical sense gets developed. Therefore, he or she tends to think of anything with a logical mind and this will help him or her to understand the problems properly. When the problems are understood properly, he or she will tend to find solutions to those problems creatively. Therefore, when one learns history, it helps him or her to develop his or her personality. Apart from these, one will gain the benefits given below by studying history. 1. Building up national reconciliation by loving humanity 5. Respecting others cultures 6. Tolerating different views
target_text: History is a subject that is studied by using different sources. It can also be learned with interest and curiosity. A student who studies history usually reads many books. By reading books, the student finds many facts, ideas, and opinions. This helps to develop critical thinking. Then the student learns to think logically and understand problems clearly. When problems are understood well, it becomes easier to find creative solutions. In this way, studying history helps to develop a persons personality. History also gives many other benefits. It helps us understand the society and the world we live in. It helps us understand the present by learning from the past, so we can build a better future. It helps us recognize our national identity. It also helps to build national reconciliation by encouraging love for humanity. In addition, history teaches us to respect other cultures and to tolerate different views.

But, it seems that the Polonnaruwa kingdom fell into a track of gradual decline due to the political disorders emerged after the demise of King Parakramabahu I. Weak rulers came to power During a period of 29 years from the year 1186 in which King Parakramabahu Is demise took place and to the year 1215 in which the downfall of Polonnaruwa kingdom occurred, 12 rulers came to power from time to time. target_text: Polonnaruwa rose with Vijayabahu I defeating Cholas and Parakramabahu I rebuilding irrigation, but declined after Parakramabahu I (1186). Causes: 1. chapter: 6.Historical Knowledge and Its Practical Application
topic: Grade 10: Protecting the Environment
source: If the members in any society need to maintain their society in a desirable manner they should protect their environment. We have to face numerous adversaries if the environment is destroyed. There is the danger of getting shortage of water due to cutting trees. Crops may be less productive due to infertility of soil as a result of soil erosion. All these bad results have to be borne up by ourselves. Our ancient rulers who were constantly conscious of the importance of protecting the environment, acted to impose laws on that. In the Anuradhapura plate inscription, made by king Mihindu IV, an order had been given forbidding clearing forests on high lands. There is a possibility of eroding soil and land slides due to clearing the forest on high lands. On the other hand it is a direct cause for drying water sources. There is an inscription, made by king Udaya III (935  938 A.D.), at a place called Buddhannehela in Anuradhapura. In that inscription there is a note as Wal Wala Dandupath Nokapanu Isa. Its meaning is that trees in the forests should not be cut. According to the rules in the Kondawattawan inscription, made by king Kashyapa V, government officers had been ordered to act
target_text: A sustainable society depends on protecting the environmentdeforestation causes water shortages and soil erosion, reducing crops. Ancient Sri Lankan kings enforced strict laws: Mihindu IV banned clearing highland forests to prevent landslides and drying springs; Udaya III (935938 AD) forbade cutting forest trees (Wal Wala Dandupath Nokapanu Isa, Buddhannehela inscription); Kashyapa V ordered officials to follow rules for tree,wood management (Kondawattawan inscription). Laws also protected wild animals and soil. Ancient organized environmental protection shows wise, respectful citizenshipwe should follow their example of living in harmony with nature for thousands of years.

Buddhist clergy advised rulers and managed temple taxes (1st2nd centuries AD). Chieftains: Rich merchants, naval captains, doctors, teachers. task: narrate
chapter: 3. Evolution of Political Power in Sri Lanka.
topic: Grade 10: The Concept of State
source: The king, created by the centralized political power, is an extremely powerful person. T herefore, the king had appeared before the people in a majestic form. The rulers thought their duty was developing the country economically, providing security to the countrymen, and enhancing spiritual development of the people. Providing security on the occasions of threats from foreign enemies, developing the economy by constructing irrigation and enhancing peoples spirituality by building religious places were some examples. The history provides evidence to prove how even kings such as Elara and Nisshankamalla, who had foreign origin, acted in the above described way and won the trust of the people. Different concepts about kingship developed according to the actions of the king and the way that they attempted to appear. Accordingly, concepts like Devathwa, Bodhisathwa, Parvatharaja, Veerathwa, and Chakravarthi were applied to the kings. Most frequently the king appeared either as a Bodhisathwa or as a god. The king Kutakanna Abhaya, who made the inscription in a rock in Dambulla temple, has introduced him by the name of Nareshvara (Naraisera). Its meaning is god Eeshwara for humans. Kin
target_text: In ancient Sri Lanka, centralized kingship created powerful rulers who appeared majestic, tasked with economic growth, security, and spiritual enhancement. Kings like Elara and Nisshankamalla, despite foreign origins, earned trust through irrigation, security, and religious support. Kings adopted titles like Devathwa (god-like, e.g., Kutakanna Abhaya as Nareshvara), Bodhisathwa (just ruler, inspiring trust), Parvatharaja (mountain king, e.g., Kashayapa I as Kuwera), or Chakravarthi (world dominator, e.g., Nisshankamalla as Kalinga Chakrawarthi). These titles showed superiority, encouraging exemplary rule. Kingship passed from father to son (e.g., Kawantissa to Dutugemunu, Vasabha to Vankanasikatissa) or brother to brother (e.g., Devanampiyatissa to his brothers), with conflicts like Kashayapas usurpation when succession deviated. The king, as Mahaparumaka or Maha Raja, led unified Parumakas post-centralization. King Dutugemunu (161137 BC) united the country, saved Mathota harbor, supported Buddhism, and organized Parumakas, showing statesmanship by honoring Elaras tomb and feeding workers at Ruwanweli Stupa. King Vasabha (67111 AD) built 16 large tanks, systematized taxes, divided provinces, and restored temples to meet South Indias grain demand. King Vijayabahu I (10551110 AD) defeated Chola rule, protected by Budhal Na in Ruhuna, restored temples, and shifted the capital to Polonnaruwa to leverage Trincomalees trade, ensuring territorial integrity and sovereignty.

topic: Grade 10: Political Condition in Sri Lanka when the Portuguese Arrived
source: There was a commercial and military importance to Sri Lanka in the Asian affairs of the Europeans as Sri Lanka was a significant centre situated close to the sea trade routes in the Indian Ocean. The Portuguese, who had recognized this country as Seilan, knew that this country produced cinnamon of good standard. T herefore, the Portuguese paid their attention to Sri Lanka within a short time period after they had come to India. In the month of March in1505, the king of Portugal sent Francisco De Almeida appointing him as the viceroy in East. Finding Seilan (Sri Lanka) was one among the instructions that had been given to Almeida by the Portuguese ruler. His purpose was achieved when Lorenzo De Almeida, a son of Francisco De Almeida, reached this country accidentally after being caught in a tempest. When Lorenzo De Almeida arrived in this country in 1505, there was a conducive background set for a foreign invasion as there was not a political unity in Sri Lanka. Though king Parakramabahu VI had united the country and created a political stability by establishing Kotte Kingdom, that unity collapsed after a certain time of his demise. Therefore, when the Portuguese arrived, there were
target_text: When Portuguese arrived (1505, Lorenzo de Almeida), Sri Lanka lacked unity: Kotte (largest), Sitawaka, Kandy, Jaffna kingdoms. Kotte divided in Vijayaba Kollaya (1521): Bhuvanekabahu VII (Kotte), Mayadunne (Sitawaka), Rayigam Bandara (Rayigama)weakening resistance. Kotte allied with Portuguese for protection against Mayadunne; Bhuvanekabahu VII assassinated (1550), Dharmapala (puppet, Catholic convert) ceded kingdom to Portuguese (deed of gift). Sitawaka (Mayadunne, Rajasinghe I) fiercely anti-Portuguese, conquering Kandy temporarily, Mulleriyawa victory (1600 Portuguese dead), but collapsed after Rajasinghe Is death (1593). Portuguese controlled coasts, Jaffna (1621 after Sankili conflicts), but failed in Kandy: Danture (1594, Pero Lopes de Sousa killed), Balana (1602), Randeniwala (1630, Constantinu de Sa killed), Gannoruwa (1638, Diogo de Melo killed)Kandyan support, defection of low-country troops, guerrilla tactics, natural defenses.

chapter: 8.Kandyan Kingdom
topic: Grade 10: Administrative Structure of the Kandyan Kingdom
source: The highest position of the administrative hierarchy of the Kandyan kingdom was held by the king. There were two Adikarams for the kings assistance. Politically, they were second only to the king. There was a royal council to discuss the important affairs of the kingdom. Adikarams, Disawes, Maha Lekams, and Badde Lekams participated in this council, which was headed by the king. The administration of the kingdom had been divided into several sections. Service in the palace (Maha Vasala Sevaya) was under the direct rule of the king; the provincial ruling structure (Rata Wasama) was afoot through Maha Adikarams, and under the supervision of the king; Maha Badda alias tax structure which was under the relevant sectional heads and administration of temples and Devalas were the major administrative sections. Kings Power : · The king was the one who possessed the highest power in the country. · Maintaining the peace in the country and providing protection were the kings major tasks. · The king was expected to act in accordance with the tradition and customs. · The king held the authority as the highest court in the country and had the power to order death sentence to a convict. · The
target_text: King at apex with absolute power (peace, protection, highest court, death sentences). Two Adikarams (second in power) assisted; royal council (Adikarams, Disawes, Lekams) advised. Sections: Palace service (direct king, Nilames like Gajanayaka, Diya Wadana); Provincial rule (Rata,Disawa21 areas, Rate Rala,Disawa heads, down to Vidanes); Badde (caste tasks, e.g., Madige transport, Kuruwe tuskers); Temples,Devalas (Mahanayaka Theros, Diya Wadana Nilame for Tooth Relic). Judiciary: King supreme, officers by rank.
